site.title: MedikQuantis
site.tagline: Cardiology clinical calculators validated in Iberian populations.

home.heading: Calculators
home.subheading: Pick a calculator to get started.

notfound.heading: Page not found
notfound.body: The resource you are looking for does not exist or has moved.

common.disclaimer: This calculator is informational and does not replace consultation with a healthcare professional.

cha2ds2vasc.title: CHA₂DS₂-VASc
cha2ds2vasc.subtitle: Stroke risk in non-valvular atrial fibrillation

fields.vascularDisease: Vascular disease (prior MI, PAD, aortic plaque)

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the probability that you will have a stroke in the next year given your atrial fibrillation and other risk factors.
patient.result_low: Your stroke risk is low. According to European guidelines, anticoagulation is not recommended on this basis alone.
patient.result_moderate: Your stroke risk is moderate. It is worth speaking with your cardiologist to assess whether anticoagulation would benefit you.
patient.result_high: Your stroke risk is high. Guidelines recommend starting oral anticoagulation to reduce it significantly.
questions: Which anticoagulant do you recommend for me and why?
What follow-up tests will I need while on treatment?
What is my bleeding risk according to the HAS-BLED score?

timi.title: TIMI
timi.subtitle: 14-day death / MI / urgent revascularization risk in UA/NSTEMI

fields.threeOrMoreCadRiskFactors: ≥ 3 CV risk factors (HTN, DM, dyslipidaemia, FHx CAD, smoking)
fields.aspirinInLast7Days: Aspirin use in the last 7 days
fields.severeAnginaInLast24h: ≥ 2 episodes of severe angina in last 24 h

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the risk of a serious complication over the next 2 weeks because of your cardiac event. It helps the team decide whether early coronary angiography is appropriate.
patient.result_low: Your risk is low. A conservative approach with non-invasive testing if needed is appropriate.
patient.result_moderate: Your risk is intermediate. Guidelines recommend considering an early invasive strategy.
patient.result_high: Your risk is high. An urgent invasive strategy and cardiology assessment are required.
questions: When are you planning the angiogram?
Which medications will I take in the next days, and why?
How can I recognise if my symptoms get worse?

grace.title: GRACE
grace.subtitle: In-hospital mortality risk in acute coronary syndrome

killipClass.I: I — no signs of heart failure
killipClass.II: II — basal crackles or third heart sound (S3)

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the risk of a serious complication during this admission for acute coronary syndrome. It helps the cardiology team decide how urgently you need a coronary angiogram.
patient.result_low: Your risk is low. A conservative strategy with close monitoring is acceptable, with invasive workup reserved for recurrent symptoms.
patient.result_moderate: Your risk is intermediate. Guidelines recommend an early invasive strategy within the first 24 hours.
patient.result_high: Your risk is high. An urgent invasive strategy (under 2 hours) and cardiology evaluation are required.
questions: When will the angiogram be done and why now?
What treatments will I need afterwards?
When can I go home and with what precautions?

heart.title: HEART
heart.subtitle: Risk stratification for chest pain in the emergency department

ecg.significant_st_depression: Significant ST depression or other ischaemic change

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the risk that your chest pain is caused by a serious cardiac problem during the next 6 weeks. It helps the emergency doctor decide whether it is safe for you to go home or whether admission for observation is needed.
patient.result_low: Your risk is low. You can probably be discharged with outpatient follow-up.
patient.result_moderate: Your risk is intermediate. Observation and a second troponin test will probably be needed before a decision.
patient.result_high: Your risk is high. You need urgent cardiology assessment and possibly a coronary angiogram.
questions: Which risk factors can I modify (blood pressure, cholesterol, smoking, weight)?
Should I come back if the pain returns?
When will I have my next follow-up?

ehra.title: EHRA
ehra.subtitle: Symptom classification in atrial fibrillation
ehra.question: How severe are the patient's symptoms?

classes.IIa: Mild symptoms — normal daily activity not affected
classes.IIb: Moderate symptoms — normal daily activity not affected but patient troubled by symptoms
classes.III: Severe symptoms — normal daily activity affected
classes.IV: Disabling symptoms — normal daily activity discontinued

patient.intro: This classification measures how bothersome your atrial-fibrillation symptoms are in daily life. It helps your cardiologist decide whether treatment to control your heart rhythm is needed.
patient.result_low: Your symptoms are mild or absent. Symptom-directed treatment is probably not required; the focus stays on stroke prevention.
patient.result_moderate: Your symptoms bother you, although you can still go about daily life. It is worth discussing rhythm or rate control options with your cardiologist.
patient.result_high: Your symptoms affect your daily life. Guidelines recommend active treatment to restore a normal heart rhythm.
questions: What options do I have to improve these symptoms?
Would I benefit from cardioversion or ablation?
How can I recognise if my symptoms get worse?

orbit.title: ORBIT
orbit.subtitle: Bleeding risk in atrial fibrillation (validated in the DOAC era)

fields.reducedHemoglobinOrHematocrit: Reduced hemoglobin (< 13 g/dL male / < 12 g/dL female) or hematocrit (< 40 % male / < 36 % female)
fields.bleedingHistory: Prior bleeding (gastrointestinal, intracranial, or hemorrhagic stroke)

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the probability of major bleeding in the next year if you take anticoagulation, based on a registry validated in the era of modern anticoagulants (DOACs).
patient.result_low: Your bleeding risk is low. This is not a reason to avoid anticoagulation if it is indicated.
patient.result_moderate: Your bleeding risk is intermediate. Reversible factors should be reviewed and follow-up made more frequent.
patient.result_high: Your bleeding risk is high. This does not preclude anticoagulation but it does require close follow-up.
questions: Can my antiplatelet be stopped, or is it essential?
Do I have anaemia? Should it be treated before starting anticoagulation?
When should we recalculate my bleeding risk?

hasbled.title: HAS-BLED
hasbled.subtitle: Bleeding risk in patients with atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation

fields.abnormalRenalFunction: Abnormal renal function (creatinine ≥ 2.3 mg/dL or dialysis/transplant)
fields.abnormalLiverFunction: Abnormal liver function (cirrhosis, bilirubin > 2× normal or transaminases > 3× normal)

patient.intro: This calculator estimates the probability that you will have a major bleeding event in the next year if you take anticoagulants.
patient.result_low: Your bleeding risk is low. This is not a reason to avoid anticoagulation if it is indicated.
patient.result_moderate: Your bleeding risk is moderate. Reversible factors should be reviewed and follow-up made more frequent.
patient.result_high: Your bleeding risk is high. This does not preclude anticoagulation but requires close follow-up and addressing modifiable factors.
questions: Which of my risk factors can I reduce (blood pressure, drugs, alcohol)?
How often should we review my bleeding risk?
What bleeding warning signs should I watch for?
